title: SEGMENT 14 — The discovery void: why no new classes for thirteen years

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The discovery void: why no new classes for thirteen years
I want to spend a segment on a part of this story that doesn't get nearly enough attention in the lay coverage of resistance: the pharmaceutical pipeline broke.
From the late 1980s through the early 2000s, essentially no new antibiotic classes were brought to market.
The discovery void.
The history is worth knowing.
The "golden age" of antibiotic discovery ran from roughly 1940 to 1970.

In those three decades, almost every major class of antibiotic we still use was found: penicillins, cephalosporins, aminoglycosides, tetracyclines, macrolides, glycopeptides, sulfonamides, chloramphenicol, rifamycins, polymyxins.
After about 1970, the rate of new-class discovery dropped sharply.
The 1970s and 1980s saw a handful of additions — carbapenems (a beta-lactam subclass), fluoroquinolones (an entirely new class derived from nalidixic acid).
And then, between 1987 and 2000, there were essentially no new structural classes of antibacterial drugs approved.
Thirteen years.
The pipeline went dry.
Why?
Three reasons, all economic.
First, the easy molecules had been found.
The natural-product chemistry of soil Streptomyces and related organisms had been heavily mined for decades.
New screens of soil isolates kept rediscovering the same compounds.
The yield of genuinely new chemistry from traditional screening had collapsed.
Second, the economics didn't work.
Antibiotics are short-course drugs — you take them for a week or two and stop.
They cure the patient.
Compare that to a statin or an antidepressant or a blood-pressure medication, which a patient might take for thirty years.
The lifetime revenue per drug is dramatically lower for antibiotics.

And the best new antibiotics — the ones reserved for resistant infections — get used as little as possible to preserve their efficacy, which is the right clinical move but a terrible commercial proposition.
Stewardship is the opposite of marketing.
No other drug class has this property.
Third, most large pharmaceutical companies exited antibiotic R&D in the 1990s and early 2000s.
Pfizer closed its antibacterial discovery group.
So did Eli Lilly.
So did Bristol-Myers Squibb.
So did Roche.
The institutional knowledge dispersed; the chemistry libraries went unused; the field shrank to a handful of mid-size companies and academic groups.
The result was that just as resistance was accelerating — ESBLs in the 1990s, carbapenemases in the 2000s, MRSA epidemics, VRE — the pipeline of new drugs had run dry.

We have spent the last two decades trying to repair this market failure with various policy interventions: pull-incentive prizes, push-incentive grants like CARB-X, the 21st Century Cures Act's expedited pathways, the UK's subscription model for paying for novel antibiotics.
Some progress.
Not enough.
The fundamental mismatch — short-course curative drugs, used as little as possible — remains.
